coredata.dc:title: Alcoholic hydroxyl functionalized partially reduced graphene oxides for symmetric supercapacitors with long-term cycle stability
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yong
Wen, Guangwu
Fan, Shan
Ma, Wenhui
Li, Shuhua
Wu, Tao
Yu, Zhaochuan
Zhao, Baoru
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, thin-walled 3D alcoholic hydroxyl functionalized partially reduced graphene oxides (APRGs) with high alcoholic hydroxyl content are easily prepared using graphene oxide (GO) dispersions via an eco-friendly one-step hydrothermal method. A small amount of Tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane (THAM) is used as the alcoholic hydroxyl source, 3D structural modifier, nitrogen doping and reducing agents, at the same time. The externally introduced alcoholic hydroxyl groups cannot only improve the wettability of the APRGs samples, but also increase their specific capacitances. Most importantly, because the chemical nature of the alcoholic hydroxyl group is stable, it imparts the APRGs sample excellent cycle stability. The APRG-30 sample with high alcoholic hydroxyl content (9.34 at%) in the wet state exhibits a high specific capacitances of 260.2 F g-1 at 0.3 A g-1 in 6 M KOH aqueous electrolyte with an excellent rate capability of 89.8% at 10 A g-1. In particular, a specific capacitance increasing of 4.7% after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g-1 is obtained, demonstrating its outstanding long-term cycle stability. These results imply that the APRG-30 sample is suitable for use as supercapacitor electrode materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alcoholic hydroxyl
Functionalized
Nitrogen doping
Graphene oxides
Supercapacitor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD, (b) Raman And (c) FT-IR of GO, PRG and APRGs.
(a) XPS survey spectra of GO, PRG and APRGs, (b) C1s XPS spectra of APRG-30, (c) N1s XPS spectra of APRG-30 (d) O1s XPS spectra of GO, (e) O1s XPS spectra of PRG, (f) O1s XPS spectra of APRG-30.
SEM images of (a) GO, (b) PRG, (c) APRG-10, (d) APRG-20, (e) APRG-30 and (f) APRG-40.
TEM and HRTEM images of APRG-30.
BET adsorption-desorption curves (a) and pore size distributions (b) of PRG and APRGs.

Electrochemical performance of the supercapacitors: CV curves of (a) APRG-30 at different scan rate, (b) PRG and APRGs at 50 mV s-1 and GCD curves of (c) APRG-30 at different current densities, (d) PRG and APRGs at 1 A g-1.

(a) Specific capacitances measured at different current densities, (b) Ragone plots, (c) EIS results of PRG and APRGs. Inset in (c) shows the expanded high-frequency region of the plots, (d) Long-term cycling stability of APRG-30 measured at 10 A g-1. Inset in (d) shows CV curves of APRG-30 at 50 mV s-1 before and after 10,000 cycles.

Electrochemical performance of APRG-30 before and after 10,000 cycles: (a) GCD curves measured at1 A g-1, (b) Specific capacitances measured at different current densities, (c) Ragone plots, (d) EIS curves.
Illustration of the possible reaction mechanism of the APRGs.
The proposed reversible Faradaic redox reactions in APRGs samples.
XRD and Raman spectra data of samples.
The elemental contents and quantitative analysis of O1s XPS data of GO, PRG and APRGs (at.%).
Summary of BET, total pore volume and most probable pore size for PRG and APRGs.

head.ce:title: Alcoholic hydroxyl functionalized partially reduced graphene oxides for symmetric supercapacitors with long-term cycle stability

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Graphene Functional Materials Research Laboratory, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Graphene Functional Materials Research Laboratory, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo, 255000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, thin-walled 3D alcoholic hydroxyl functionalized partially reduced graphene oxides (APRGs) with high alcoholic hydroxyl content are easily prepared using graphene oxide (GO) dispersions via an eco-friendly one-step hydrothermal method. A small amount of Tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane (THAM) is used as the alcoholic hydroxyl source, 3D structural modifier, nitrogen doping and reducing agents, at the same time. The externally introduced alcoholic hydroxyl groups cannot only improve the wettability of the APRGs samples, but also increase their specific capacitances. Most importantly, because the chemical nature of the alcoholic hydroxyl group is stable, it imparts the APRGs sample excellent cycle stability. The APRG-30 sample with high alcoholic hydroxyl content (9.34 at%) in the wet state exhibits a high specific capacitances of 260.2 F g-1 at 0.3 A g-1 in 6 M KOH aqueous electrolyte with an excellent rate capability of 89.8% at 10 A g-1. In particular, a specific capacitance increasing of 4.7% after 10,000 cycles at 10 A g-1 is obtained, demonstrating its outstanding long-term cycle stability. These results imply that the APRG-30 sample is suitable for use as supercapacitor electrode materials.

ce:section.ce:para: With the aggravation of the world energy crisis, a large number of green energy conversion devices have been developed [1–3]. Therefore, how to efficiently store these energy sources has become an urgent problem for human beings. Supercapacitor is a new type of energy storage device, whose performance is between battery and traditional physical capacitor. It has the following outstanding features, such as high power density, short charging time, long cycle life, wide operating temperature range, high work safety coefficient and instantaneous high current discharge [4]. Therefore, supercapacitor has been widely used in many fields, and has attracted the attention of researchers all over the world [5,6]. According to its energy storage mechanism, supercapacitor can be divided into two types. One is electrical double layer capacitor (EDLC), and the other is a pseudocapacitor. EDLC use the large specific surface area of electrode materials, which are generally carbon materials, to absorb/desorption electrolyte ions to store energy. Therefore, the EDLC has excellent cyclic stability, but its specific capacitance is usually small [7,8]. Pseudocapacitor uses the reversible redox reaction occurring on the surface of the electrode material to store energy, and the electrode material is generally transition metal oxide or conductive polymer. Consequently, pseudocapacitor has large specific capacitance, but its cyclic stability is very poor [9,10]. In summary, the electrode material is a key factor in determining the electrochemical performance of the supercapacitor. It is well known that transition metal oxides and conductive polymers may not be used as electrode materials for next-generation supercapacitors due to their inherently large electrical resistance and poor cycle stability. Therefore, we believe that how to add reversible redox reaction site on the surface of carbon material while retaining its cycle stability, and then increase its pseudocapacitance becomes a feasible method to solve the problem of energy storage of the next-generation supercapacitor.

GO is an intermediate product in the preparation of graphene. It has massive oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface [11]. These oxygen-containing functional groups cannot only improve the wettability but also increase the pseudocapacitance of the materials [12–14]. However, in the process of preparing GO, the graphene sheets are seriously damaged by the oxidant, resulting in low conductivity, which seriously restricted its use as an electrode material [15]. At present, GO is generally used as a precursor material after being excessively reduced or heavily doped with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, boron, phosphorus, sulfur, etc., and then used as an electrode material for supercapacitor. During these reactions, a large number of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of GO are removed to restore its electrical conductivity [16–18].

We think this is a waste of pseudocapacitance of oxygen-containing functional groups, and also increase the manufacturing cost of supercapacitors. The reason is that the acidic oxygen-containing functional groups on the GO surface, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl, can produce Faraday redox reaction in the alkaline electrolyte to improve the pseudocapacitance of the materials. In addition, quinone can yields pseudocapacitance in acid electrolyte, and carbonyl group can store or release electrons without ion exchange [19,20]. Different from the oxygen-containing functional groups mentioned above, epoxy group cannot increase the pseudocapacitance or raise the wettability of the material, and too many epoxy groups can seriously impair the conductivity of the material. Based on the above analysis, we want to eliminate detrimental epoxy groups with energy-saving and environment-friendly hydrothermal method, partially reduce graphene oxide, and introduce some useful oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of graphene sheets, so as to enhance its electrochemical properties.

There are many methods to increase the supercapacitor properties of a material by introducing an oxygen-containing functional group into the carbon skeleton. For example, oxygen-containing precursor activation, electrochemical oxidation, plasma, etc. However, the production costs of these methods are generally high and require relatively expensive production equipment [21–24]. Therefore, we hope to use organic compounds containing both amino and hydroxyl groups to perform nitrogen doping and partial reduction of GO under energy-saving and environment-friendly hydrothermal conditions, and introduce more hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of graphene sheets at the same time.

Hydroxyl groups can be divided into phenolic hydroxyl group and alcoholic hydroxyl group. Phenolic hydroxyl refers to the hydroxyl group that is directly connected to the carbon atoms in the benzene ring, while alcoholic hydroxyl refers to the hydroxyl group that is connected to the carbon atoms in the aliphatic hydrocarbon. The two hydroxyl groups show different properties due to different connection modes. For instance, although the hydrophilicity of both hydroxyl groups is very good, the chemical properties of alcoholic hydroxyl group are relatively stable, so the materials functionalized with alcoholic hydroxyl groups will have better cyclic stability. Moreover, the alcoholic hydroxyl group will undergo an acid-base neutralization reaction with the KOH solution during the electrochemical reaction, thus introducing a large amount of negative charge on the surface of the material and thereby reducing the diffusion resistance of the materials.

THAM molecule contains an amino group and three alcoholic hydroxyl groups. Therefore, we hope that the molecular chain of THAM can be connected to the surface of graphene sheets by the reaction between amino and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the GO. It can be seen from Scheme 1 that the amino group in the THAM molecule can promote the decomposition of the epoxy functional group through the bimolecular nucleophilic substitution reaction. When the carbonyl group and the aldehyde group react with the amino group, unstable alcohol-amine intermediates will be formed first, and then the alcohol-amine will remove a water molecule and finally form a C=N structure. The adjacent carboxyl group and hydroxyl group will have an esterification reaction, and the products of this reaction will react with the amino group to form amide compounds, and then form a more stable five-membered heterocyclic pyrrole structure by decarbonylation. Therefore, we believe that after the hydrothermal reaction with THAM, the sheet structure of GO is repaired due to the entry of nitrogen-containing functional groups, and the number of oxygen-containing functional groups was correspondingly reduced. In addition, the tail chains of THAM molecules are also introduced into graphene sheet interlayer by these reactions. These tail chains can inhibit the π-π stacking between the GO sheets to prevent the stacking and agglomeration of the graphene sheets, and adjacent Hydrogen bonds are also formed between the alcoholic hydroxyl groups on the tail chain of the sheets. All these factors can increase the space between the graphene layers. In particular, these alcoholic hydroxyl groups will increase the wettability and specific capacitance of the materials.

In this work, we present a simple method to synthesize thin-walled 3D alcoholic hydroxyl functionalized partially reduced graphene oxides (APRGs) with an energy-saving and environment-friendly one-step hydrothermal process use GO and THAM as reaction reagent. In this reaction system, THAM is not only reducing agent, nitrogen dopant but also serve as alcohol hydroxyl donor and internal structure regulator of the sample. Consequently, the as-prepared APRGs samples have large specific surface area, thin-walled 3D porous structures, restored sheet structures by nitrogen atom and high alcoholic hydroxyl content. These properties are beneficial to improve the electrochemical performance of the sample. For practical applications, symmetric binder-free supercapacitors are assembled, which display exceptional cycle stability and excellent supercapacitors performance.

GO was synthesized from flake graphite following a modified Hummers’ method [25]. APRGs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method with THAM and GO as reaction reagent. Typically, 160 mg GO was added to 80 mL of deionized water, and after ultrasonic treatment for 1 h, 2 mg mL-1 of GO aqueous solution was prepared. THAM (10 mg, 20 mg, 30 mg, 40 mg) was added to the above aqueous GO solution, respectively. The prepared mixed solution was magnetically stirred for 30 min, and then transferred into a 110 mL Teflon lined autoclave and then the autoclave was placed in an oven at 453 K for 12 h. After the reaction was completed and cooled to room temperature, the product was taken out and filtered with filter paper. Then the filtered samples were freeze-dried under vacuum conditions for 48 h. The APRG-10, APRG-20, APRG-30, APRG-40 were nominated based on the mass of THAM. For comparison, we also prepared partially reduced graphene oxides (PRG) under the same reaction conditions without the addition of THAM.

The crystallographic structures of the materials were obtained at room temperature using X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns on a DX-2700 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. The surface chemical functional groups of the samples were characterized by FT-IR spectra using an AVATAR-370 spectrometer. The Raman spectra of the materials were analyzed using an Invia Raman microscope (Renishaw, England). The chemical compositions of the samples were measured through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. The nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms of the samples were examined by a 3H-2000PS1 analyzer. The specific surface areas of the samples were determined via the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The morphologies and microstructures of the samples were investigated by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4300) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100).

A symmetrical two-electrode configuration was used to evaluate the electrochemical property of the samples. The working electrode was fabricated by directly pressing the wet state sample on the nickel foam sheet at 10 MPa for 30 s without the adding of conductive additives and binders. Subsequently, the working electrode was immersed in 6 M KOH aqueous electrolytes for 12 h. The symmetrical supercapacitor was assembled by using two nearly identical electrodes separated by a piece of cellulose film and then placed in a stainless steel CR2025 type coin cell.

All electrochemical tests were performed through a CHI760E electrochemistry workstation (Chenhua Instruments, Shanghai, China) in 6 M KOH at room temperature. The electrochemical performance of the samples was studied by cyclic voltammograms (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The specific capacitance (Cs) of the electrode derived from charge/discharge curves was calculated by using the equation C
s = 2(I × Δt/m × ΔV), in which I is the constant discharge current (A), Δt stands for the discharge time (s), m corresponds to the mass of the sample in a single electrode (g), and ΔV represents the potential range, excluding IR drop during the discharge process (V). The energy density (E, Wh kg-1) and power density (P, W kg-1) were calculated based on equations E = C
s
ΔV
2
/8 and P = E/Δt.

The structural changes from GO to PRG and APRGs are investigated by XRD analyses. As shown in Fig. 1 a and Table 1 , GO shows a sharp and strong diffraction peak at 2θ = 10.22° (001) with an interlayer spacing of 8.69 Å. This big interlayer spacing can be assigned to the existence of substantial oxygen functional groups and crumple on the GO sheets. After the hydrothermal process with or without THAM, the obtained PRG and APRGs samples show very weak and broad peaks at vicinity of 22.5° (Table 1), close to that of graphite. These changes in the pattern of PRG and APRGs indicate that the GO is partially reduced, as well as the formation of few-layer stacked graphene nanosheets. Notably, the interlayer spacing of APRGs samples is larger than that of PRG samples, which indicate that the THAM molecular chains could effectively inhibit the agglomeration of the graphene sheets. Compared with PRG sample, the (001) characteristic peak width of APRGs samples is significantly increased, indicating that the APRGs sheets have more lattice defects and vacancies due to the N doping and alcoholic hydroxyl functionalization [26]. These lattice defects and vacancies can increase the contact point between the electrode material and electrolyte ions and then improve the electrochemical performance of the material. Moreover, the XRD pattern of APRGs exhibits a more obvious weak diffraction peaks than that of PRG at around 43.5°, corresponds to the (100) reflection, suggesting that the APRGs have looser packed structure [27].

Raman spectra are utilized to further examine the structural changes of the prepared samples. Raman spectra of graphene materials usually display two prominent peaks around 1350 cm-1 and 1590 cm-1, corresponding to the D band and G band, respectively. The former associated with the disordered or structural defects of sp3 hybridization carbon atoms, while the latter is rooted in the flawless graphene structure [28]. It can be seen in Fig. 1b and Table 1, the D band and G band of APRGs are shifted to a higher region comparing with that of GO and PRG, which can be attributed to the elimination of oxygen containing functional groups and N doping in graphene sheets [29,30]. Generally, the intensity ratio of D band to G band (ID/IG) is used to evaluate the graphitization degree in graphene materials. Evidently, APRGs have larger ID/IG ratio than that of GO and PRG, suggesting the presence of bonding disorders and vacancies, due to the N doping (Table 1). At the same time, the increased ID/IG values also indicate the transformation of sp3 carbon in GO to sp2 carbon in APRGs, which will greatly improve the conductivity of the APRGs. Based on the above analysis results, it can be concluded that the THAM molecules have been successfully connected to the surface of the graphene sheet and GO is partially reduced after the hydrothermal process. Therefore, we believe that the electrochemical properties of APRGs samples will be much better than PRG samples due to the doped N atoms and the functionalized alcoholic hydroxyl groups.

As shown in Fig. 1c, the absorption peaks representing O-C-O (epoxy group), C-OH (hydroxyl), –COOH (carboxyl group) and C=O (carbonyl group) in the FT-IR spectra of GO appear at the wave number of ∼1060 cm-1, ∼1396 cm-1, ∼1622 cm-1 and ∼1739 cm-1, respectively [31]. The broad peak at ∼3418 cm-1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration absorption peak of –OH in the physisorbed water, and also indicating extensive oxidation of graphite. For APRGs and PRG samples, the intensities of these characteristic peaks for oxygen containing functional groups are obviously reduced, which prove that GO is partially reduced after the hydrothermal process. Moreover, there are two kinds of new peaks appear at ∼1081 cm-1 and ∼1561 cm-1, which can be ascribed to the C-N stretching vibrations and N-H bending vibration in APRGs samples, respectively. The FT-IR spectra analysis further confirms the N doping and partial reduction of GO after the hydrothermal process.

The high-resolution O1s spectra of GO, PRG and APRGs (Fig. 2d–f) can be deconvoluted into five peaks at around 530.55 (quinone O), 531.86 (O=C-OH, carboxyl group), 532.22(C=O, carbonyl group), 532.69 (C-O, epoxy) and 533.43 eV (C-OH, hydroxyl groups), respectively [37]. The atomic percentages calculated from the O1s spectra of the as-prepared samples are listed in Table 2. After the hydrothermal reaction, the atomic percentages of quinone O and carboxyl group have risen slightly. The atomic percentages of both carbonyl and epoxy groups are reduced. Especially the epoxy functional group which is harmful to the electrochemical performance of the material, its atomic percentage decreased from 20.98 to 2.22 at%. The ternary ring structure of the epoxy functional group leads to the insufficient overlap of the atomic orbitals and thus forms a bent bond structure. This kind of curved bond structure increases the ring tension inside the epoxy functional group and makes it have certain reactivity. When the temperature of the hydrothermal reaction reaches 453 K, the epoxy functional group can undergo ring-opening reaction with water to form two hydroxyl groups. When there are amino-containing organic compounds with strong nucleophilic ability in the reaction system, the amino group can promote the decomposition of the epoxy functional group through the bimolecular nucleophilic substitution reaction. Therefore, the atomic percentages of hydroxyl functional groups in APRGs (∼9.34 at%) are significantly higher than that of GO (2.71 at%) and PRG (4.79 at%). The oxygen-containing functional groups retained in the sample and the hydroxyl functional groups introduced after hydrothermal reaction with THAM can effectively improve the wettability and pseudocapacitance effect of the materials [38]. Based on the results of the XPS analysis, we confirm that GO is partially reduced by the hydrothermal reaction with THAM and the content of the epoxy functional group which is detrimental to the electrochemical performance of the material is drastically reduced. With the success of nitrogen doping, the THAM molecular chains are attached to the surface of the graphene sheets. Therefore, the APRGs samples contain a large amount of alcoholic hydroxyl groups and a small amount of nitrogen-containing functional groups. These heteroatom functional groups can enhance the wettability and electrical conductivity of the material and thereby improve the EDLC capacitance of the material. In addition, these heteroatom functional groups can also greatly increase the pseudocapacitance of the materials by Faraday reaction.

To investigate the surface morphologies and structure of GO, PRG and APRGs, SEM and TEM are carried out. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the GO sheets are in densely stacked state attribute to the van der Waals force between the layers. As can be seen from Fig. 3b–f, both PRG and APRGs samples have interconnected thin-walled 3D porous network, which is composed of corrugated graphene sheets and has random open pores [39,40]. The pore size of these pores is distributed in the range of submicrometer to several micrometers. Notably, in a simple hydrothermal reaction without the addition of THAM, the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the GO sheets are partially removed. Therefore, the high-concentration partially reduced graphene sheets will assemble into hydrogels due to the π-π stacking effect [41]. However, when THAM reacts with GO under hydrothermal conditions, its molecular chains will be introduced into the graphene sheets. These molecular chains will effectively inhibit the π-π stacking effect and form hydrogen bonds between the adjacent graphene sheets, thus expanding the space between graphene sheets. Therefore, compared with PRG, APRGs has a larger porous network.

Low magnification TEM image (Fig. 4 a) also indicates that the thin-walled 3D porous structures of the as-prepared APRG-30 sample is composed of randomly stacked, thin and corrugated graphene sheets, which can be assigned to the defects and the THAM molecules that are introduced into the graphene sheets. Meanwhile, the darker part in Fig. 4a is the intersection of the graphene sheets, the other higher-transparency parts represent the few or single graphene layers that do not participate in the stacking [39]. The corresponding HRTEM image (Fig. 4b) suggests that the edge of APRG-30 is consisted of several graphene sheets. The aforementioned results demonstrate that the as-prepared APRGs samples have thin-walled 3D porous structures. This kind of structure can facilitate the contact between the electrode material and the electrolyte ions, so as to improve the EDLC capacitance of the material. Furthermore, it can increase the reaction probability between the electrolyte and the sites with pseudocapacitance effect, so as to improve the pseudocapacitance of the materials.

The 3D porous networks of the PRG and APRGs samples are further characterized by the N2 absorption/desorption measurement. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the PRG and APRGs samples exhibit characteristic type IV absorption/desorption curves, with a remarkable hysteresis loop at a relative pressure (P/P0) from 0.4 to 1, implying the existence of copious mesopores [42]. The mesopores maybe originate from the stacking between the graphene sheets. It can be seen in Table 3 that the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of APRGs are much higher compare to that of PRG. In addition, the total pore volume of the APRGs samples increases with the increase of THAM dosage. These results prove that the THAM molecules play an important role in enhancing the specific surface area and porosity of the samples. Fig. 5b and Table 3 display the pore size distribution curves of PRG and APRGs samples based on the Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods. Evidently, APRGs contain larger volume of mesopores (2–50 nm) and micropores (<2 nm) than that of PRG. Besides, both PRG and APRGs have some macropores with the size larger than 50 nm [43]. And most importantly, all the pores are larger in diameter than hydrated K+ (∼0.66 nm) and hydrated OH− (∼0.60 nm), which makes it easier for ions to enter the pores. The specific surface analysis results show that the APRGs samples have large specific surface area and rich pore structures, which will help to improve the rate capability and EDLC performance of the material in the application of supercapacitors.

The electrochemical properties of the APRGs and PRG as binder-free electrode materials for supercapacitors are evaluated in a two electrode configuration cell in 6 M KOH electrolyte. Fig. 6 a presents the CV curves of APRG-30 electrode in the potential range from 0 to 1 V and the scan rates from 5 to 100 mV s-1, respectively. All curves display quasi-rectangular shape with broad redox humps between 0 and 0.8 V, which suggests the coexistence of EDLC capacitance and pseudocapacitance [44]. Moreover, the CV curves still maintain quasi-rectangular shape and the loop areas gradually become bigger with increasing the scan rate, denoting fast charge propagation and good rate performance for APRG-30 electrode [45].

From the results of FT-IR and XPS analysis of the samples, it is found that a large amount of alcoholic hydroxyl functional groups and a small amount of nitrogen-containing functional groups are introduced on the surface of the APRGs sample after hydrothermal reaction with THAM. In addition, some oxygen-containing functional groups remain on the surface of the sample after the hydrothermal reaction (Table 2). These heteroatom functional groups cannot only improve the conductivity and wettability of the material, but also enhance the pseudocapacitance of the sample. Since 6 M KOH electrolyte is used in this paper, we believe that acidic nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups are the main source of pseudocapacitance in APRGs electrodes, especially the large number of alcoholic hydroxyl functional groups. Besides, the carbonyl functional group can store and release electrons without ion exchange. Based on the above analysis, we conclude that the pseudocapacitance of the APRGs sample is mainly produced by the Faraday reaction present in Scheme 2 . Fig. 6b shows the CV curves of APRGs and PRG samples at a scan rate of 50 mV s-1. Evidently, the loop areas of APRGs are larger than that of PRG, revealing the larger specific capacitance of APRGs due to the larger specific surface areas and the thin-walled 3D porous structures with better pore size distribution. It can be noted that the broad redox humps are more manifest in APRGs electrodes, which can be ascribed to the pseudocapacitance generate by the introduced alcoholic hydroxyl groups and nitrogen-containing groups.

Fig. 6c displays the GCD curves of APRG-30 electrode at various current densities ranging from 0.3 to 5 A g-1. The GCD curves are all show nearly symmetrical shapes with a slight distortion in slopes, further demonstrating the coexistence of EDLC behavior and pseudocapacitive effect, consisting with the CV analyses [46]. Furthermore, the IR drop of the discharge curves is almost negligible, which verifies the low internal resistance and good electrochemical reversibility of the APRG-30 electrode. Fig. 6d compares the GCD curves of APRGs and PRG electrodes at 1 A g-1. It is obvious that the discharging time for APRGs is longer than that of PRG, indicating their higher energy storage capacity [47]. Consequently, the electrochemical properties of the sample are significantly improved after the hydrothermal reaction with THAM.

The specific capacitances of APRGs and PRG samples at different current densities are calculated based on the GCD curves. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the specific capacitances of the APRGs samples are significantly higher than that of the PRG. At current densities of 0.3, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5 and 10 A g-1, the specific capacitances of APRG-30 electrode are calculated as 260.2, 257.2, 255.3, 246.8, 243.3, 239.3 and 233.7 F g-1, with an excellent capacitance retention of 89.8% from 0.3 to 10 A g-1. However, PRG electrode only exhibits 196.1, 191.9, 186.4, 180.3, 176.4, 170.4 and 160.3 F g-1 at these current densities, with about 81.7% capacitance retention. Apparently, the rate capability of APRG-30 electrode is better than that of PRG. This can be ascribed to the introduced alcoholic hydroxyl groups and nitrogen-containing functional groups, which will improve the ion diffusion and wettability of the carbon materials [48].

The Ragone plots of APRGs and PRG based symmetric supercapacitors in 6 M KOH electrolyte are depicted in Fig. 7b. Evidently, the supercapacitors assembled by APRGs can deliver higher energy density compare to that of PRG. The energy densities of APRG-30 electrode are calculated as 9.0, 8.8, 8.7, 8.3, 8.0, 7.7 and 6.8 Wh kg-1, while the power densities are 74.8, 124.4, 247.0, 491.8, 731.8, 1201.0 and 2289.4 W kg-1 at current densities of 0.3, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5 and 10 A g-1, respectively. Furthermore, as the power density of the device increases, its energy density decreases slightly. Therefore, we think that the obtained energy densities of APRG-30 based supercapacitors can meet the requirements of practical application. In contrast, PRG based device can merely delivers relatively low energy densities of 6.8, 6.6, 6.3, 5.9, 5.6, 5.2 and 4.2 Wh kg-1 at power densities of 74.7, 124.1, 246.6, 486.5, 719.5, 1166.3 and 2167.9 W kg-1 at the same conditions.

To further illustrate the improved electrochemical performance of APRGs electrodes, the electrochemical transfer kinetics process and conductivity of electrode materials are measured by EIS. The Nyquist plots of APRGs and PRG samples are provided in Fig. 7c. In high frequency region, the intercept of the Nyquist plots with the real axis of APRGs are much smaller than that of PRG (inset of Fig. 7c), indicating the APRGs electrodes have lower equivalent series resistance [49]. In addition, an obvious semicircle can be seen on the Nyquist plots of all samples in the high frequency region, which indicates the existence of pseudocapacitance. The semicircle diameters of the APRGs sample are significantly smaller than that of PRG, illustrating that the APRGs samples have smaller charge transfer resistance. At low frequency region, the slope of the APRGs electrodes are steeper compare to that of PRG electrode elucidates the faster ion diffusion rate on the electrode surface and better charge-discharge behavior of the APRGs. At the intermediate frequency region, APRGs have smaller 45° Warburg region, denoting the faster ion diffusion rate inside the APRGs samples [50]. The EIS results can be assigned to the introduced alcoholic hydroxyl groups and nitrogen-containing functional groups and thin-walled 3D porous structures of APRGs, which can not only reduce the contact resistance between the electrode material and the electrolyte, but also provide a large number of channels for the transfer of electrolyte ions.

As is known, cycling life is a prerequisite for the practical application, so the supercapacitor based on the APRG-30 is chose to test the cycling life with 10,000 cycles at a high current density of 10 A g-1. As shown in Fig. 7d, the cycle life curve of APRG-30 electrode shows a slow rising trend. After 10,000 charge and discharge cycles, its specific capacitance value reaches 104.7% of its initial value, verifying its outstanding long-term cycle stability. By comparing the CV curves of APRG-30 before and after 10,000 cycles at 50 mV s-1, we find that the loop area of the CV curve increased, implying that the specific capacitance increased (inset of Fig. 7d). The intensity of the broad redox peak in the range of 0–0.4 V decreased slightly, which suggests that some unstable oxygen-containing groups (such as carboxyl and phenolic hydroxyl) are decomposed during the cycle test. However, the intensity of the broad redox peak in the range of 0.4–0.8 V is significantly increased, indicating that the pseudocapacitance of APRG-30 still display an increasing trend after 10,000 cycles.

In order to confirm the cause of the capacitance increase, we conduct a series of tests on the APRG-30 based symmetric supercapacitor after 10,000 cycles of testing. The GCD curves of APRG-30 electrode (Fig. 8 a) before and after 10,000 cycles at 1 A g-1 can draw a similar conclusion with the CV curves inset of Fig. 7d. Fig. 8b compares the specific capacitance of the APRG-30 sample at different current densities before and after 10,000 cycles. Before the cycle test, the specific capacitance of APRG-30 at 0.3 and 10 A g-1 are 260.2 and 233.7 F g-1, respectively. After 10,000 cycles, the corresponding values rise to 276.8 and 244.7 F g-1, respectively, and the specific capacitance retention rate is still as high as 88.4%. Fig. 8c displays the Ragone plots of APRG-30 electrode before and after 10,000 cycles. The results demonstrate that the energy density of APRG-30 has been significantly improved after cyclic testing. Before the cycle test, the energy densities of the samples are 9.0 Wh kg-1 and 6.8 Wh kg-1, while the power densities are 74.8 and 2289.4 W kg-1 at 0.3 and 10 A g-1, respectively. However, after the cycle test, the corresponding energy densities rise to 9.6 Wh kg-1 at 75.1 W kg-1 and 7.1 Wh kg-1 at 2290.3 W kg-1, respectively. Nyquist plots of APRG-30 before and after 10,000 cycles are shown in Fig. 8d. Obviously, after 10,000 cycles, the Nyquist plot has a smaller semicircle in high frequency region and a steeper line in low frequency region. This indicates that the charge transfer resistance and ion diffusion resistance of the APRG-30 electrode is reduced after 10,000 cycles of testing [51,52]. Based on the above analysis results, we believe that the increase of capacitance is due to the introduced alcoholic hydroxyl groups. This is because the alcohol hydroxyl group has a low chemical activity and will not react with KOH to form salt at room temperature, but it can increase the hydrophilicity of the material. With the progress of cyclic test, the alcoholic hydroxyl group is gradually activated, so as the charge-discharge cycle proceeds, more and more alcoholic hydroxyl groups can contribute to the pseudocapacitance of the material, so the electrochemical activity and ionic conductivity of the sample are significantly improved.

The good electrochemical properties of APRGs electrode may be based on the following reasons: (1) The lattice defects and vacancies in APRGs can increase the contact point between the electrode material and electrolyte ions and then improve its electrochemical performance. (2) The externally introduced alcoholic hydroxyl group cannot only increase the wettability and pseudocapacitance of the sample, but also improve the cycle stability and rate performance of the material. (3) The doped N atoms can repair the in-plane structure of graphene and improve its electrical conductivity. It can also improve the pseudocapacitance of the material by Faraday reaction. (4) The thin-walled 3D porous structures of APRGs can supply effective pathway for electrolyte ions, so as to improve the EDLC capacitance of the material. Therefore, we believe that these factors together contribute to the excellent electrochemical performance of APRGs.

In conclusion, APRGs with thin-walled 3D porous structures and high alcoholic hydroxyl content have been prepared by an eco-friendly one-step hydrothermal method with THAM as the alcoholic hydroxyl source, 3D structural modifier, nitrogen doping and reducing agents, at the same time. In consequence of the synergistic effect of alcoholic hydroxyl, doped N atoms and thin-walled 3D porous structures, the APRGs based binder free symmetric supercapacitors exhibit high specific capacitance and excellent rate capability. Most important of all, the APRGs electrodes display outstanding long-term cycle stability. Before cycles test, the specific capacitance of APRG-30 at 0.3 and 10 A g-1 are 260.2 and 233.7 F g-1, respectively. After 10,000 cycles at a high current density of 10 A g-1, the corresponding values rise to 276.8 and 244.7 F g-1, respectively. Moreover, the charge transfer resistance and ion diffusion resistance of the APRG-30 electrode is become smaller after 10,000 cycles. These results prove that the APRGs samples can be used as electrode materials of energy storage device.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support from The Research and Development Project of Basic Research Business Fees of Provincial Higher Education Institutions in Heilongjiang Province in 2018 (Special Subject of Plant Food Processing Technology, No. YSTSXK201870 and YSTSXK201871), The National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51303087), Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (No. QC2015057) and the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (No:135309110).
